sound_type: rhythmic gnawing woody percussion -- the cyclic crunch of iron-reinforced incisors cutting into wood, persistent and metronomic, the first acoustic element that represents sustained labor rather than communication
ecosystem: riparian -- streamside forest, the transitional zone where trees meet water, the beaver's workshop

species_pair.sound_1: tail slap (degree 61) -- percussive warning, E=4, pond/creek

coordinates.description: Carkeek Park / Piper's Creek reference; common on freshwater streams throughout western Washington. Chewing most audible along streamside corridors where mature deciduous trees stand within 50 meters of water. Same geographic range as degree 61 but different microhabitat: the riparian corridor (among the trees) rather than the open pond.

taxonomy_note.description: Second consecutive Castoridae entry. Same species as degree 61 (Castor canadensis), different sound. Species-pair #2 after Northern Flicker (degrees 21-22). No new class, order, or family -- all introduced at degree 61. Castoridae count increases to 2 entries. This is the first mammalian species-pair and the first species-pair in 40 degrees.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Same binomial as degree 61. Castor from Greek kastor (beaver). canadensis = 'of Canada.' The common name 'beaver chewing' distinguishes this acoustic entry from the 'American Beaver' tail slap entry at degree 61. Same species, different sound, different name in the engine.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Castor -- same genus as degree 61. Only living genus in Castoridae. Two species: C. canadensis (North America) and C. fiber (Eurasia).
taxonomy_note.order_note: Rodentia -- same order as degree 61. Second consecutive Rodentia entry. Largest mammalian order (~2,200 species). The gnawing that defines this degree is the defining behavior of the entire order: Rodentia comes from Latin rodere, 'to gnaw.' The beaver's chewing is the order's etymological act.
taxonomy_note.class_note: Mammalia -- same class as degree 61. Second consecutive Mammalia entry. The engine's mammalian representation doubles from 1 entry to 2 entries (1.6% to 3.2% of 63 total entries).
taxonomy_note.family_position: Castoridae's second entry at degree 62. The species-pair structure means Castoridae now has as many entries as Anatidae (degrees 58-59) and Bombycillidae (degrees 31-32) -- all families with exactly 2 entries from back-to-back degrees.

song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: NOT a vocalization. The chewing sound is the acoustic byproduct of iron-reinforced incisors cutting into wood. Each bite produces a broadband transient (~200-4,000 Hz) with most energy in the low-to-mid range (300-1,500 Hz). The dominant frequency depends on wood species and trunk diameter -- softer woods produce lower-pitched sounds, harder woods produce higher-pitched crunching.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: 0.2-0.5 seconds per bite; 10-30 minutes per gnawing session; cumulative hours per tree
song_metrics.note_structure: RHYTHMIC SEQUENCE: Each bite is a discrete transient (crunch/scrape), repeated at a steady cadence of approximately 30-60 bites per minute. The rhythm is determined by the biomechanics of the gnawing action. No melodic structure, no tonal content, no pitch variation within a session. The pattern is metronomic.
song_metrics.note_types: Single type: the gnawing bite. Minimal variation. Each bite sounds similar to the next. The variation comes from wood density and trunk geometry rather than behavioral choice. This is the most acoustically uniform element in the engine.
song_metrics.phrase_structure: No phrase structure in the musical sense. The gnawing is a continuous rhythmic sequence with no development, no climax, no resolution. It begins when the beaver starts gnawing and ends when it stops. The only structural feature is the work-rest-work pattern: 15-20 minutes of gnawing, a pause to listen and groom, then resumption.
song_metrics.timbre: Woody, crunching, mechanical. Each bite sounds like a short scraping impact -- wood fibers fracturing under the force of iron-reinforced enamel. Has been compared to a heavy rasp on hardwood or a muffled chisel strike. Lacks the sharp crack of the tail slap. Lacks the resonance of woodpecker drumming. A utilitarian sound.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=3 -- moderate, persistent, audible at 30-80 meters in quiet nocturnal conditions. Quieter than the tail slap (E=4) but far more sustained. The sound rewards proximity: you must be near the working beaver to hear it clearly. The reduced amplitude reflects the incidental nature of the sound -- it is not produced to be heard.
song_metrics.tempo: Approximately 30-60 bites per minute, remarkably steady. Faster on small branches (softer wood, less resistance), slower on large trunks (harder wood, more repositioning). The metronomic quality is the gnawing's most distinctive acoustic feature.
song_metrics.context: FUNCTIONAL LABOR. The gnawing occurs during tree felling (food harvest and dam material), branch processing (cutting downed trees into transportable lengths), and bark stripping (feeding). Primarily NOCTURNAL -- most gnawing occurs between dusk and dawn with peak activity in the first hours after sunset. Not a signal, not communication -- the incidental sound of work.
song_metrics.regional_variation: NO variation. The gnawing sound is a physical action (incisors cutting wood), not a learned behavior. There are no dialects, no regional patterns, no cultural learning. Every beaver gnawing on the same species of tree at the same trunk diameter produces essentially the same sound.
song_metrics.key_property: THE RHYTHMIC GNAW -- the 63rd acoustic element. The first acoustic element in the engine that represents SUSTAINED LABOR rather than communication. Rhythmic, persistent, nocturnal, incidental. The sound of a tree being felled by an animal whose incisors never stop growing and never stop sharpening. Species-pair complement to the tail slap (degree 61). Warning (61) yields to work (62). Explosion yields to rhythm.

physical_description.overall: Same species as degree 61. See SPS-061 for full physical description. The relevant anatomy for degree 62 is the INCISORS and the gnawing biomechanics, not the tail.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: For degree 62, the diagnostic features are: (1) ORANGE INCISORS -- iron-rich enamel, self-sharpening, continuously growing at 4-5 cm/year. (2) GNAW MARKS on trees -- the hourglass-shaped notch around the trunk, with wood chips accumulated at the base. (3) TRIPOD STANCE -- the beaver stands on hind legs with flat tail bracing, working at chest height.
physical_description.incisors_detail: The acoustic instrument of degree 62. Upper and lower incisors, each approximately 20-25 mm wide and 2-3 cm visible length (but extending deep into the jaw). Iron-rich enamel on the front surface (orange, Mohs ~5.5) is harder than the dentine on the back surface, creating a self-sharpening chisel edge through differential wear. Grow continuously at ~4-5 cm per year. Can fell trees up to 30 cm diameter. The defining rodent adaptation scaled to ecosystem-engineering proportions.
physical_description.gnawing_biomechanics: The beaver approaches the tree, stands on hind legs with tail as tripod brace. Turns head sideways to position incisors against the trunk. Upper incisors anchor; lower incisors do the cutting work, moving in an arc that levers chips of wood from the trunk. Each bite removes a chip 1-5 cm long. The beaver rotates around the trunk, creating an hourglass notch. Head position alternates sides to maintain even cutting.
physical_description.field_id: At night along streamside corridors: listen for a steady, rhythmic crunching/scraping sound -- chunk-chunk-chunk -- at approximately 30-60 beats per minute. Look for: fresh wood chips at the base of trees near water, hourglass gnaw marks on tree trunks, characteristic pointed stumps. The gnawing sound is the primary nocturnal evidence of beaver activity in riparian zones.

nodes.title: Species-Pair #2 -- Same Beaver, Different Sound
nodes.description: The second species-pair in the engine. The American Beaver appears at degree 61 (tail slap, E=4, warning, pond/creek) and degree 62 (chewing, E=3, labor, riparian). Same species, two acoustic modes. Mirrors the Northern Flicker pair (degrees 21-22, voice + drumming) forty degrees later. The first mammalian species-pair. Bird pair then mammal pair. The engine's structural device for documenting multi-modal acoustic species.

nodes.title: The Rhythmic Gnaw -- 63rd Acoustic Element
nodes.description: Steady chunk-chunk-chunk of iron-reinforced incisors cutting into wood. Broadband transients ~200-4,000 Hz, peak 300-1,500 Hz. E=3. Tempo ~30-60 bites/minute. Sessions lasting 10-30 minutes. The engine's FIRST acoustic element that represents SUSTAINED LABOR rather than communication. Not a signal, not a call -- the incidental sound of work. Primarily nocturnal. Audible at 30-80 meters in quiet conditions.

nodes.title: First Functional Acoustic Element -- The Sound of Work
nodes.description: Every previous acoustic element (degrees 0-61) served a communicative function: territory, mating, alarm, contact, identity. The beaver chewing communicates nothing. It is the incidental acoustic byproduct of tree felling -- the sound that results from labor. The engine's acoustic vocabulary expands from signals and songs to include the sounds of work. The 63rd element is the first that is not meant to be heard.

nodes.title: Nocturnal Returns -- First Night Sound Since Degree 4
nodes.description: The beaver chewing is primarily nocturnal -- most gnawing occurs between dusk and dawn. This is the first primarily nocturnal acoustic element since the Northern Spotted Owl (degree 4), a gap of 58 degrees. For 58 degrees, the engine documented a predominantly diurnal soundscape. The gnawing pulls the engine back into darkness. The night soundscape reconnects after the longest daytime stretch in engine history.

nodes.title: The Night Acoustic Environment
nodes.description: At night: wind drops, bird song ceases, water sounds dominate, insects contribute high-frequency layers. The beaver's low-frequency gnawing sits beneath the insects and above the water. In this quieter acoustic environment, the steady chunk-chunk-chunk becomes atmospherically dominant despite its moderate amplitude. The gnawing becomes part of the creek's night voice.

nodes.title: Iron-Reinforced Self-Sharpening Incisors
nodes.description: The acoustic instrument of degree 62. Orange iron-rich enamel (Mohs ~5.5) on front surface, softer dentine on back. Differential wear creates a self-sharpening chisel edge -- the more the beaver gnaws, the sharper the teeth become. Grow continuously at ~4-5 cm/year. Can fell trees 15 cm diameter in ~30 minutes, up to 30 cm diameter with sustained effort. The defining rodent adaptation (Rodentia from Latin rodere, 'to gnaw') scaled to ecosystem-engineering proportions.

nodes.title: Tree Felling -- Selection, Technique, the Fall
nodes.description: Beavers preferentially fell soft-wooded deciduous trees (cottonwood, alder, willow, maple) within 30 meters of water. Gnaw an hourglass notch around the trunk from a tripod stance. 15 cm diameter in ~30 minutes. Prefer trees leaning toward water. The hourglass cut causes the tree to fall away from the beaver. A family of 4-8 beavers fells 200-300 trees per year. Selective harvest, not random destruction.

nodes.title: Ecosystem Engineering Part 2 -- Destruction as Creation
nodes.description: Degree 61 documented what the beaver BUILDS (dams, ponds, lodges). Degree 62 documents what the beaver DESTROYS (fells trees, gnaws bark, opens canopy). Creation and destruction are the same process. The felled tree becomes dam material. The canopy gap stimulates regrowth. The stump resprouts. Every tree destroyed becomes raw material for creation. The gnawing sound is simultaneously a tree dying and a wetland being born.

nodes.title: Riparian Zone -- The Beaver's Workshop
nodes.description: Degree 61's habitat was the pond (the beaver's living room). Degree 62's habitat is the riparian zone (the beaver's workshop). The transitional strip where forest meets water. Among the most ecologically productive habitats in any landscape. Beavers maintain riparian zones in perpetual disturbance -- felling trees, opening gaps, stimulating regrowth. The shift from pond to riparian is the shift from the beaver's product to the beaver's workplace.

nodes.title: Five Consecutive Aquatic-Primary Species (58-62)
nodes.description: Wood Duck (58, E=3) -> Mallard (59, E=5) -> Grebe (60, E=4) -> Beaver tail slap (61, E=4) -> Beaver chewing (62, E=3). The aquatic quartet becomes a quintet. Energy curve: 3-5-4-4-3 -- symmetric arc peaking at the Mallard. Five degrees, three orders, two classes. The longest sustained habitat affinity in engine history. The aquatic era extends.

nodes.title: E=3 -- Persistent vs. Explosive
nodes.description: The chewing is E=3 (lower than the tail slap's E=4). Audible at 30-80 meters vs. 200-500 meters. But the chewing persists for minutes to hours while the tail slap lasts <0.1 seconds. The tail slap dominates the moment; the chewing dominates the night. E=3 reflects incidental amplitude (not produced to be heard) rather than secretive behavior. The energy rewards proximity and patience.

nodes.title: Flicker Pair Echo -- 40 Degrees Later
nodes.description: Northern Flicker at degrees 21-22 (voice + drumming, same bird, Aves). Beaver at degrees 61-62 (tail slap + chewing, same mammal, Mammalia). 40 degrees separate the two species-pairs. The Flicker split vocalization from percussion. The beaver splits warning percussion from labor percussion. Both document multi-modal acoustic species. Bird pair then mammal pair. The engine's structural rhyme.

nodes.title: Spotted Owl Echo -- 58 Degrees of Daylight
nodes.description: Northern Spotted Owl (degree 4) was the last primarily nocturnal element. Beaver chewing (degree 62) is the first primarily nocturnal element since. 58 degrees of predominantly diurnal soundscape between them. The owl called from old-growth canopy; the beaver gnaws at the streamside trunk. Both heard only in darkness. The night reconnects.

nodes.title: Wood Modifiers -- Woodpecker and Beaver
nodes.description: Pileated Woodpecker (54) excavates cavities in standing wood. Beaver (62) fells entire trees. Both physically modify wood. Different scale: the woodpecker removes cubic centimeters, the beaver removes entire trunks. Both create habitat through wood destruction. The woodpecker works during the day on vertical surfaces; the beaver works at night at the base. Two wood-modification strategies, avian and mammalian.

nodes.title: The Pickwick Parallel
nodes.description: Both defined by rhythmic energy. Pickwick's driving, propulsive sound mirrors the beaver's metronomic gnawing. Both represent concentrated effort over a finite period that leaves lasting marks: Pickwick's brief career (2010-2016) like a beaver's gnawing session on a single tree. Intensity of effort matters more than duration. Both leave marks disproportionate to the time spent.

nodes.title: Second Consecutive Original CSV Species
nodes.description: Degree 62 is the second consecutive original CSV species (after degree 61 broke the 16-degree substitution streak). Two degrees in a row without substitution -- the first time since degrees 43-44 that consecutive degrees used original CSV species. The engine appears to have exited the amphibian belt permanently.

cross_references.relationship: SPECIES-PAIR #2. Same species (Castor canadensis), different sound. The tail slap (degree 61, E=4, percussive warning, pond/creek) and the chewing (degree 62, E=3, functional labor, riparian). Warning vs. work. Explosion vs. rhythm. The engine's second species-pair after the Northern Flicker (degrees 21-22) and the first mammalian species-pair. 40 degrees between the two pairs.

FIRST SPECIES-PAIR (degrees 21-22). Voice + drumming -- same bird, two acoustic modes. The beaver pair at 61-62 mirrors this structure 40 degrees later: same mammal, two acoustic modes. Bird pair then mammal pair. Vocalization/percussion split (Flicker) vs. warning/labor split (Beaver). The engine's structural device for multi-modal species.

FIFTH CONSECUTIVE AQUATIC SPECIES. The aquatic quartet (58-59-60-61) becomes a quintet at degree 62. The grebe swims in the ponds the beaver builds; the beaver gnaws the trees that become the dams. Energy arc: 3-5-4-4-3 -- symmetric, peaking at the Mallard.

NOCTURNAL RETURNS. The beaver chewing is the first primarily nocturnal acoustic element since degree 4 -- a gap of 58 degrees. The owl called from old-growth canopy at night; the beaver gnaws at streamside trunks at night. The night soundscape reconnects after the longest daytime stretch in engine history.

WOOD MODIFIERS. Both physically modify wood to create habitat. The woodpecker excavates cavities in standing trees (cubic centimeters); the beaver fells entire trees (cubic meters). Different scale, same ecological function: wood destruction as habitat creation. One avian and diurnal, one mammalian and nocturnal.

Second consecutive beaver entry in the SPS engine. The chewing represents the beaver's primary ecological activity: tree harvest for food and construction material. Nocturnal, riparian, year-round. The engine's mammalian representation increases from 1 entry to 2 (1.6% to 3.2%).

Ecosystem engineering through selective tree harvest. Beaver gnawing creates canopy gaps (sunlight reaches forest floor), stimulates shrub regrowth (stump sprouting), provides dam material (branches and trunks), and maintains riparian zones in a state of perpetual productive disturbance. 200-300 trees felled per family per year.
